Summary\n\nBoth CLIs' `--capture browser` path uses a **time-based** wait to decide when `DOCS_modelChunk` data is \\\"ready\\\" for extraction. This is a heuristic, not correctness:\n\n- **Rust** returns on the **first non-empty poll** → observable flake: large docs with >1 model chunk sometimes return a truncated capture (only the first chunk).\n- **JS** waits a **flat 8000 ms** after `domcontentloaded` → works for docs that finish streaming within 8 s but has no guarantee for larger docs.\n\nThe right design is \\\"**poll until chunks stop changing**\\\" — i.e. quiescence detection with a configurable stability window and a hard timeout. Both CLIs should converge on that design.\n\n## Observed flake (Rust)\n\nReinstalled Rust `0.3.8` in isolation, ran `--capture browser` against the same Google Doc 5 times back-to-back. The doc has enough content to be streamed across multiple `DOCS_modelChunk` assignments (the editor emits chunks progressively for bigger documents — size matters here, not content).\n\n\`\`\`\nrun 1: chunks=1 cid_urls=39 blocks=26 images=15 text_bytes=6489   # TRUNCATED\nrun 2: chunks=2 cid_urls=39 blocks=66 images=39 text_bytes=11553  # complete\nrun 3: chunks=1 cid_urls=39 blocks=26 images=15 text_bytes=6489   # TRUNCATED\nrun 4: chunks=1 cid_urls=39 blocks=26 images=15 text_bytes=6489   # TRUNCATED\nrun 5: chunks=2 cid_urls=39 blocks=66 images=39 text_bytes=11553  # complete\n\`\`\`\n\nThe image map (`cid_urls=39`) loads fully in both variants — only the `DOCS_modelChunk` stream is affected. When the capture is truncated, **more than half the document content is silently missing** from the archive (`document.md`, `document.html`, and `images/`).\n\nJS `1.7.16` in isolation on the same doc returned 39 images / 280 markdown lines **5 times out of 5** — so on documents that finish streaming well under 8 s, JS's fixed wait happens to cover the race. On a doc that takes >8 s it would fail the same way.\n\n## Root-cause pointers\n\n**Rust — `rust/src/gdocs.rs`, `wait_for_google_docs_model_chunks` (~L1035)**:\n\n\`\`\`rust\nwhile started.elapsed() < GDOCS_EDITOR_MODEL_WAIT {  // 30 s cap\n    let model_data = ...;  // Runtime.evaluate → read window.__captured_chunks\n    if !model_data.chunks.is_empty() {\n        return Ok(model_data);  // <-- returns on FIRST non-empty, not on quiescence\n    }\n    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(250)).await;\n}\n\`\`\`\n\nEvery 250 ms the loop asks the page \\\"how many chunks do you have?\\\" and returns the moment the answer is ≥1. If Google Docs emits chunk 1 at t≈1.2 s and chunk 2 at t≈1.8 s, the 1.25 s poll will happily return with just chunk 1.\n\n**JS — `js/src/gdocs.js`, `captureGoogleDocWithBrowser` (~L413)**:\n\n\`\`\`js\nawait page.goto(editUrl, { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded', timeout: 60000 });\nawait waitForPage(page, waitMs);   // waitMs defaults to 8000 ms — FLAT SLEEP\nconst modelData = await evaluateOnPage(page, () => {\n  const chunks = [...(window.__captured_chunks || [])];\n  ...\n});\n\`\`\`\n\nThe `Object.defineProperty(window, 'DOCS_modelChunk', { set })` hook installed at L523 keeps recording chunks throughout the 8 s sleep, so late chunks *are* captured — but only if they arrive within 8 s. There is no verification that the stream has settled; a slow network or a big doc will silently lose tail chunks.\n\n## Proposed fix (both CLIs — same algorithm)\n\nReplace the time-based wait with a quiescence loop, parameterized by a **stability window** and a **hard timeout**:\n\n\`\`\`\nstability_window = 1500 ms   (chunks must stop changing for this long)\nhard_timeout     = 30000 ms  (give up if still churning)\npoll_interval    = 250 ms    (how often to snapshot)\n\`\`\`\n\nAlgorithm (pseudocode, applies to both JS and Rust):\n\n\`\`\`\nlast_fingerprint = None\nstable_since     = None\ndeadline         = now + hard_timeout\nwhile now < deadline:\n    fingerprint = (chunks.length, total_chunk_payload_size)\n    if fingerprint == last_fingerprint and fingerprint.0 > 0:\n        if stable_since is None: stable_since = now\n        if now - stable_since >= stability_window: return chunks   # quiesced\n    else:\n        stable_since     = None\n        last_fingerprint = fingerprint\n    sleep(poll_interval)\nraise TimeoutError(\\\"DOCS_modelChunk stream did not quiesce within hard_timeout\\\")\n\`\`\`\n\nNotes:\n\n- `total_chunk_payload_size` in the fingerprint catches the edge case where chunk count stays the same but an existing chunk's contents grow (observed occasionally — Docs appends to the same array). Just counting chunks isn't sufficient.\n- The `fingerprint.0 > 0` guard prevents declaring quiescence before the first chunk arrives.\n- `stability_window` of 1.5 s is a starting point — inspired by what a human \\\"looks idle\\\" threshold would be. Make it env-configurable (`WEB_CAPTURE_GDOCS_STABILITY_MS`) so large/slow docs can bump it.\n- `hard_timeout` of 30 s matches Rust's current `GDOCS_EDITOR_MODEL_WAIT`. Keep it env-configurable too (`WEB_CAPTURE_GDOCS_MAX_WAIT_MS`).\n- The return must never happen purely from elapsed time; the only success path is \\\"no changes for `stability_window`\\\". A timeout return should either error (strict mode) or return with a warning log (\\\"returned possibly-incomplete capture; set WEB_CAPTURE_GDOCS_MAX_WAIT_MS higher\\\").\n\n## Why this also removes JS's hidden flake\n\nJS's current 8000 ms wait works for the docs in existing test fixtures, but that's coincidence. On a slow network, an 8 s wait after `domcontentloaded` may also return before chunk streaming finishes. Once both CLIs adopt quiescence-based waiting, behavior becomes deterministic across network speed, doc size, and machine load — small docs return in ~1.5 s, large ones take as long as they take, nothing is silently truncated.\n\n## Testing\n\nAdd a deterministic regression test that **forces non-trivial chunking** without depending on a specific private document:\n\n1. **Synthetic public fixture.** Create a public Google Doc in CI's fixture set where the body is large enough to reliably emit >1 `DOCS_modelChunk`. A practical threshold is ~8-12 KB of document text: enough paragraphs of Lorem ipsum, a handful of inline images (use `docs.google.com/`-hosted test pngs), no unusual formatting required. The exact cutoff Google uses for chunking is implementation-defined, but in our testing docs with `text_bytes >= 10000` reliably emit ≥2 chunks.\n\n2. **Two assertions in `gdocs_public_doc` live tests** (both languages):\n   - `chunks >= 2` (or `text_bytes >= 10000`) — forces us to actually exercise the multi-chunk path.\n   - **Stability check**: run capture N times (N=5 is enough); **image count and `text_bytes` must be identical across all runs**. This is the regression guard — if a run truncates, it'll stand out in the N-sample comparison.\n\n3. **Log the stability diagnostic.** The `parsed Google Docs editor model` info line already prints `chunks`, `cid_urls`, `blocks`, `images`, `text_bytes`. Add `poll_count` and `stable_for_ms` to it so that operational issues (\\\"this doc took 14 s to quiesce\\\") are visible in production logs.\n\n4. **No changes needed to existing test fixtures** — the issue-90 reference doc (`1f5zI2xOFpKa90v0GjamO_t7lqSdzMlaM`) is small and emits `chunks=1`, which is why it didn't surface this bug. Keep it; add the new multi-chunk fixture alongside.\n\n## Repro recipe (without private docs)\n\n1. Go to docs.google.com and create a new document.\n2. Paste Lorem ipsum until `Tools → Word count → Characters` shows ≥ 10000.\n3. Insert 4-6 `Insert → Image → Upload` blocks scattered through the doc.\n4. `Share → Anyone with the link → Viewer`. Copy the link.\n5. Put that URL in `$URL`, then:\n\n\`\`\`bash\n# Rust\ncargo install web-capture\nfor i in 1 2 3 4 5; do\n  rm -rf run$i && mkdir run$i && cd run$i\n  web-capture \\\"$URL\\\" --archive zip --capture browser 2>&1 \\\\\n    | grep \\\"parsed Google Docs editor model\\\"\n  cd ..\ndone\ncargo uninstall web-capture\n\`\`\`\n\nExpect: most runs show `chunks=2, images=<N>, text_bytes=<big>`, but a fraction show `chunks=1, images=<~half>, text_bytes=<~half>`. The fraction depends on machine speed and network — on a fast Mac (M-series, good connection) we saw 3/5 truncated runs.\n\n\`\`\`bash\n# JS (for contrast — no flake on sub-8-s docs, but same design flaw)\nnpm i -g @link-assistant/web-capture\nfor i in 1 2 3 4 5; do\n  rm -rf run$i && mkdir run$i && cd run$i\n  web-capture \\\"$URL\\\" --archive zip --capture browser > /dev/null 2>&1\n  unzip -p \\\"$(find . -name document.zip)\\\" document.md | wc -l\n  cd ..\ndone\nnpm uninstall -g @link-assistant/web-capture\n\`\`\`\n\nJS is likely to come out stable at 8 s, but increase doc size to ≥ 30-40 KB text to push past the 8 s window and you'll see it truncate too.\n\n## Environment\n\n- JS: `@link-assistant/web-capture@1.7.16` on Node 20.19.4 (package declares `node >=22 <23`).\n- Rust: `web-capture 0.3.8` (crates.io), stable toolchain.\n- Each CLI installed in full isolation (other uninstalled first) to rule out PATH shadowing.\n- macOS 24.6.0.\n\n## Related\n\n- #104 (merged) — fixed HTML rendering parity; exposed this underlying capture-completeness bug, because divergence caused by this flake was previously attributed to the rendering difference.\n- #102 — `--capture api` fidelity defects (separate issue).","closed_by":null,"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/link-assistant/web-capture/issues/106/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/link-assistant/web-capture/issues/106/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null,"pinned_comment":null}
